fragments.content: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is the most common endocrine disorder in reproductive-age women, affecting 6-12% prevalence. Characterized by hyperandrogenism (clinical: hirsutism, acne, alopecia; or biochemical: elevated testosterone), ovulatory dysfunction (oligo/anovulation manifesting as irregular menses), and polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound. Pathophysiology involves insulin resistance (present in ~70% regardless of BMI), hyperinsulinemia stimulating ovarian androgen production, altered gonadotropin secretion (elevated LH:FSH ratio), and genetic predisposition. Rotterdam criteria (2003) require ≥2 of 3 features for diagnosis. Long-term health risks include type 2 diabetes (4-fold increased risk), metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease, endometrial cancer (from unopposed estrogen), infertility, mood disorders.

Treatment tailored to patient goals. For menstrual regulation/hyperandrogenism (not seeking pregnancy): combined oral contraceptives first-line (suppress LH, increase SHBG, reduce ovarian androgens); add antiandrogen (spironolactone 50-100mg BID) if inadequate response after 6 months - must use contraception due to teratogenicity. Metformin (1500-2000mg/day) improves insulin sensitivity, may restore ovulation, beneficial for metabolic parameters but less effective for hirsutism. Lifestyle modification (weight loss 5-10%) improves all PCOS features. For infertility: letrozole first-line for ovulation induction (superior to clomiphene); clomiphene alternative; gonadotropins or IVF if oral agents fail. Metformin adjunctive for ovulation induction in some cases. Endometrial protection essential if not having regular withdrawal bleeds. Screen/treat comorbidities: glucose intolerance, dyslipidemia, hypertension, sleep apnea, depression.

Diagnosis using Rotterdam criteria requires ≥2 of 3 features after excluding other causes: (1) Clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism: hirsutism (modified Ferriman-Gallwey score ≥4-6 depending on ethnicity), severe acne, male-pattern alopecia; or elevated total/free testosterone, DHEA-S; (2) Ovulatory dysfunction: oligomenorrhea (cycles >35 days) or amenorrhea (>3 months without menses); (3) Polycystic ovaries on ultrasound: ≥20 follicles per ovary (2-9mm diameter) and/or ovarian volume >10mL. Exclusion criteria: thyroid dysfunction, hyperprolactinemia, non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (17-OHP screening), Cushing syndrome, androgen-secreting tumors (rapid onset, virilization, very high testosterone). Workup: TSH, prolactin, 17-OHP, total/free testosterone, DHEA-S, pelvic ultrasound. Consider OGTT for glucose intolerance screening given high diabetes risk.
